title: Prosperity hiding decay
content: At first glance the 17th-century Ottoman Empire still looked magnificent. Its lands spanned three continents, and the markets of Istanbul, Cairo, and Baghdad overflowed with spices, silk, coffee, and textiles flowing along vital trade routes. But beneath this glittering surface, deep cracks were forming in the empire's foundations.
next: What was quietly eating away at the empire's strength?

title: The silver shock
content: A crisis arrived from across the ocean. Floods of New World silver pouring into European markets collapsed the metal's value - and the Ottoman currency, based on silver, collapsed with it. Prices soared, money lost its worth, and ordinary citizens watched their savings evaporate as the state struggled to pay its bills.
next: Who paid the heaviest price for this economic chaos?

title: Revolt in the countryside
content: The burden fell hardest on peasant farmers. Heavy taxes, combined with corrupt local officials more interested in enriching themselves than serving the people, pushed rural communities to the brink. Discontent finally exploded into open rebellion - the Celali revolts swept Anatolia, exposing how weak the imperial center's grip on its own heartland had become.
next: Meanwhile, what became of the empire's once-feared soldiers?

title: The Janissaries turn inward
content: The Janissary corps had once been the disciplined backbone of Ottoman power - elite soldiers raised through the devshirme system and loyal only to the sultan. But over time they became a privileged, self-interested force, more focused on palace intrigue and politics than on war. Instead of defending the empire, they increasingly destabilized it.
next: If the center was weakening, who filled the gap?

title: The rise of the local lords
content: As the sultan's authority faded in the provinces, local strongmen called ayan rose to fill the vacuum. Drawn from powerful families or military backgrounds, they carved out their own regions of control, collecting taxes and commanding loyalty. The once-unified empire was quietly fragmenting into a patchwork of semi-independent power centers.
next: And what threats were pressing in from outside?

title: Squeezed on every front
content: Beyond its borders the empire was locked in constant struggle - the Safavid Empire to the east, and increasingly capable European powers to the west. Endless campaigns drained the treasury, diverting money from reform and development. The Ottomans were caught in a vicious cycle: external pressure deepened internal weakness, and internal weakness crippled their response to external threats.
next: Then came one ambitious gamble that would change everything.

title: The march on Vienna
content: In 1683 the Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha led an army of over 150,000 men toward Vienna, the heart of the Habsburg Empire. This was no raid - it was a bold bid to strike at the core of Christian Europe and push Ottoman power deep into the continent. Both sides understood the enormous stakes.
next: Could the city possibly withstand such a force?

title: Two months under siege
content: Ottoman artillery battered Vienna's walls while sappers tunneled beneath them. Inside, the vastly outnumbered defenders under Ernst Rudiger von Starhemberg endured hunger, disease, and the constant threat of a breach. For two desperate months the city held, its people knowing they were the last line of defense against Ottoman expansion into central Europe.
next: Was rescue even possible?

title: The charge that broke the siege
content: Help came from the north. King Jan III Sobieski of Poland marched a coalition of Polish, Austrian, and German forces to Vienna's aid. On September 12, 1683, they crested the Kahlenberg hills and unleashed one of the largest cavalry charges in history - Sobieski's famed winged Hussars smashing into the Ottoman lines. The army that had nearly taken the city collapsed into a full retreat.
next: What did this defeat unleash on the empire?

title: The tide turns for good
content: Vienna was the turning point. For the first time, Europeans sensed the Ottoman tide could be reversed. The victory galvanized Christian Europe into forming the Holy League in 1684, a coalition determined to drive the Ottomans out of central Europe. The empire that had seemed unstoppable now began a long, slow retreat that would last for centuries.
next: But first, a surprising burst of beauty and reform.

title: The Tulip Era
content: After the Treaty of Passarowitz brought rare peace in 1718, the empire turned inward to enjoy it. Under Sultan Ahmed III, the Tulip Era (Lale Devri) bloomed - a time when the Ottoman elite became obsessed with tulips as symbols of refinement and luxury, with rare bulbs fetching extravagant prices. Freed from war, the leadership focused on culture and gentle reform.
next: What did this peaceful interlude produce?

title: Looking west
content: Istanbul flourished with new palaces, libraries, and ornate fountains - the elegant Fountain of Ahmed III still stands before Topkapi Palace. For the first time, the Ottoman elite eagerly embraced European art, architecture, and fashion, especially from France. Ambassadors returned from Europe with new ideas, opening a two-way exchange that would slowly reshape Ottoman culture.
next: One imported idea would prove especially revolutionary.

title: The printing press arrives
content: In 1727 Ibrahim Muteferrika, a Hungarian-born Ottoman scholar, established the first Ottoman printing press. While religious texts stayed handwritten, the press could now mass-produce secular works - scientific treatises, histories, and more. It marked a turning point in how knowledge and ideas could spread across the empire.
next: But not everyone welcomed this wave of change.

title: Backlash: the Patrona Halil revolt
content: While the elite enjoyed tulip gardens and lavish parties, ordinary people faced rising taxes and hardship. To conservative factions, the westernization and extravagance of the Tulip Era looked like decadence. In 1730 the discontent erupted: the Patrona Halil revolt, led by a former Janissary, united soldiers, religious conservatives, and commoners to storm the palace and force Ahmed III from the throne.
next: Now a new and dangerous rival appeared on the empire's border.

title: Russia rising
content: The 18th century brought a formidable new adversary: Imperial Russia. Hungry for warm-water ports and control of Black Sea trade routes, Russia pushed south toward Ottoman territory. The strategic Black Sea region - and especially Crimea - became the focal point of a rivalry that would define Ottoman foreign affairs for generations, igniting a long series of Russo-Turkish wars.
next: How badly would these wars go for the Ottomans?

title: The loss of Crimea
content: The Russo-Turkish War of 1768-1774 ended in disaster with the Treaty of Kucuk Kaynarca. Russia gained access to the Black Sea, the right to 'protect' Orthodox Christians inside Ottoman lands, and influence over the Crimean Khanate. That set the stage for Russia's outright annexation of Crimea in 1783 - a humiliating loss that shook the empire to its core.
next: These defeats forced a painful realization.

title: Selim III's New Order
content: The defeats laid bare how far Ottoman armies had fallen behind modern European forces. Sultan Selim III responded by creating the Nizam-i Cedid, a 'New Order' army modeled on European training and equipment, alongside financial reforms to fund it. But the Janissaries, seeing the new army as a threat to their privileges, fiercely resisted - and their opposition crippled these early reforms.
next: By the next century, reform could no longer wait.

title: Tanzimat: reorganization
content: Facing humiliating defeats and growing rebellion, the empire chose to adapt rather than perish. The Tanzimat - meaning 'reorganization' - began. Building on Mahmud II's groundwork, Sultan Abdulmecid I issued the 1839 Edict of Gulhane, promising legal equality for all Ottoman subjects regardless of religion or ethnicity, followed by a second great reform edict in 1856.
next: How deeply did these reforms reshape the empire?

title: Building a modern state
content: The Tanzimat reforms touched nearly everything. New civil and criminal codes drawn from European models created secular courts alongside Islamic ones. Secular schools and military academies trained a new class of officials in Western methods. Railways and telegraph lines knit distant provinces to the capital, while modern ports tied the empire into global trade.
next: So why didn't modernization save the empire?

title: The debt trap
content: The reforms brought real change but also real danger. Modernization was expensive, and the empire increasingly financed it with European loans. This growing financial dependency would eventually leave the Ottomans economically beholden to foreign powers. Conservatives resented the abandonment of tradition, while in the Balkans subject peoples now wanted independence, not equality.
next: That desire for independence was about to tear the empire apart.

title: The fire of nationalism
content: A powerful new idea spread through the empire's diverse peoples: ethnic nationalism, born of European Enlightenment thought. For centuries the Ottomans had balanced many ethnic and religious groups under one rule. Now that balance tipped, as communities began to imagine futures as independent nations rather than imperial subjects. The Balkans became the epicenter of this awakening.
next: Which people would be the first to break free?

title: Greece breaks away
content: In 1821 Greek revolutionaries rose against Ottoman rule, fighting for nine years and capturing the sympathy of Europeans across the continent. Their success in 1830 sent shockwaves through the empire: it proved independence was possible. Serbia steadily won autonomy through revolts and negotiation, and Bulgaria's cultural revival stirred its own dreams of nationhood.
next: How much would these losses cost the empire's map?

title: The map redrawn at Berlin
content: The cycle of revolt, harsh repression, and foreign intervention steadily eroded Ottoman legitimacy - and Russia eagerly positioned itself as protector of the Balkans' Slavic Orthodox Christians. The Congress of Berlin in 1878 redrew the region: Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro became fully independent, and Bulgaria autonomous. The empire that once spanned three continents was visibly contracting.
next: Could a new generation of reformers reverse the decline?

title: The Young Turks
content: In 1908 a group of reformers known as the Young Turks, organized under the Committee of Union and Progress (CUP), launched a revolution. They forced Sultan Abdulhamid II to restore the constitution he had suspended for three decades, reviving parliamentary government. Their goal was to centralize authority, curb European interference, and drag the empire into the modern age.
next: Who really held power after the revolution?

title: Power behind the throne
content: A failed counter-coup in 1909 led to Abdulhamid II's deposition, replaced by the figurehead Mehmed V. Real power shifted to the CUP, dominated by a triumvirate of Enver Pasha, Talat Pasha, and Cemal Pasha. But they inherited disaster: the Italo-Turkish War cost Libya, and the Balkan Wars of 1912-1913 stripped away nearly all the empire's remaining European territory.
next: Into this weakened state came the greatest war the world had seen.

title: A fateful choice
content: Hoping to reverse its decline and reclaim lost lands, the Ottoman Empire entered World War I in 1914 on the side of the Central Powers. It was a catastrophic gamble. The winter campaign of 1914-1915 at the Battle of Sarikamish against Russia, fought in the freezing Caucasus mountains, killed tens of thousands of Ottoman soldiers through cold and starvation alone.
next: And the war tore at the empire from within as well.

title: Revolt and atrocity
content: In 1916 the Arab Revolt, backed by the British officer T. E. Lawrence and fueled by promises of independence, broke Ottoman control across the Middle East. This same period saw one of the darkest chapters in Ottoman history: in 1915 the government began the Armenian genocide, the mass deportation and death of over a million Armenians - an atrocity that still shapes international relations today.
next: How did the war finally end the empire?

title: The treaty of humiliation
content: The Ottomans ended the war on the losing side. The Treaty of Sevres in 1920 carved up the empire's remaining lands, placing vast territories under Allied control and leaving only a small piece of Anatolia under Ottoman rule. For many Turks, Sevres was not merely defeat but humiliation - and it sparked a fierce nationalist backlash.
next: From that anger rose the man who would build modern Turkey.

title: Ataturk's war of independence
content: A decorated officer named Mustafa Kemal Ataturk became the driving force of the Turkish War of Independence (1919-1923). At the grueling three-week Battle of Sakarya in 1921 his forces repelled a Greek offensive, turning the tide. Victory at Dumlupinar in 1922 shattered Greek resistance in Anatolia and led to the recapture of Izmir, proving an independent Turkish state could survive.
next: What did victory mean for the centuries-old dynasty?

title: The end of the empire
content: In November 1922 the Grand National Assembly abolished the Ottoman Sultanate, ending over six centuries of imperial rule. The last sultan, Mehmed VI, fled Istanbul. The Treaty of Lausanne in 1923 replaced the hated Sevres, recognized Turkish sovereignty, and fixed the borders of modern Turkey. On October 29, 1923, Ataturk proclaimed the Republic of Turkey, with Ankara - not Istanbul - as its capital.
next: What did the new republic do with the last symbol of the old order?

title: A new nation, a lasting legacy
content: In March 1924 Ataturk abolished the caliphate, ending the last vestige of Ottoman religious authority and signaling a firmly secular state. He launched sweeping reforms - the Latin alphabet, Western dress, a European-style civil code, and voting rights for women - known as Kemalism. The Ottoman Empire was gone, but its legacy still shapes the modern Middle East and Balkans, a reminder of how even the mightiest powers can pass.
next: From a nomadic tribe to a republic - the full Ottoman arc is complete.
